Intern: Learning to think in flows. Building the eye for how good products work.
Sketches divergent options before committing to one.
Studies how good products work
Has a curious eye for behaviour and craft.
Brings fresh examples into design conversations.
Associate Product Designer: Owns design for short flows, from larger scoped work
Asks scope-clarifying questions before starting. Doesn't begin designing on assumptions.
Includes edge cases, empty states and errors in the work, not just the happy path.
Treats microcopy as part of design.
Product Designer II: Owns whole flows end-to-end reliably without needing a lot of direction
Asks the right questions to sharpen a fuzzy brief, before solving.
Knows the common UX patterns well enough to pick the right one and adapt it.
Writes microcopy that's clear, intentional and on brand.
Proactively brings in research/data to influence design direction.
Senior Product Designer: Reframes the fuzzy briefs, and holds together multi-flow work.
Reframes ambiguous briefs into sharper problems for the team.
Fluent in UX patterns and methodologies. Aware of when to use them and when to break them.
Surfaces potential technical/business constraints early, during discovery itself
Staff Product Designer: Takes on work with no precedent to lean on.
Frames work with no precedent to lean on; where no past project gives them a reference
Defines new UX patterns and documents, when existing ones don't fit.
Designs across multi-surface, multi-user systems where the right answer is undefined.
Principal Product Designer: Deepest IC. Their framing on novel problems becomes the studio's reference.
Takes on category-defining problems no one has framed. Often work that defines new categories.
UX patterns influence people beyond the studio. Referenced by designers & client orgs.
Kills or redirects directions early with clear rationale
Decisions make future work faster.
Design Lead: Great IC. Raises framing and quality across the projects they design on / steward.
Reframes ambiguous briefs across multiple projects, and unblocks when teams are stuck.
Owns design quality across multiple projects they're stewarding on.
Coaches ideation as a skill. Grows the team's ability to reframe, diverge, and choose themselves.
Their patterns travel beyond the studio. Designers and PMs at clients adopt them.
Associate Design Director: Owns design quality across the studio's portfolio
Holds the framing standard across the projects they oversee
Owns design quality across a portfolio of projects at once.
Designers across different projects use their approach to framing as a role model.
Design Director: Sets the studio's stance on what good problem framing, UX & Ideation work looks like.
Defines what great looks like across the whole competency at Parallel
Calls which kinds of problems the studio takes on and which it doesn't.
Shapes which problem areas the competency invests in and which approaches get retired.

Intern: Building craft foundations and the eye for what works.
Typography, spacing and hierarchy in place on simple work.
Foundations of typography, spacing and hierarchy are in place.
Good judgement of craft. Able to pick between strong and weak craft decisions.
Associate Product Designer: Executes craft reliably on standard work when the visual direction is clear.
Typography, layout, hierarchy and spacing are reliably right on standard work.
Treats motion and interaction as part of design.
Visual decisions are deliberate, not default.
Product Designer II: Strong craft bar, held across projects. Supports in raising the bar.
Hits a high craft bar consistently. Type, layout, spacing, hierarchy & motion design are reliably strong.
Restraint where it helps, polish where it earns its place.
Catches their own craft inconsistencies before reviews.
Senior Product Designer: Craft level matches the best in their product category. Raises the craft bar in the studio.
Visual decisions are traceable, rooted to the problem.
Their critiques visibly change what ships.
Keeps dense, complex screens calm; craft holds on the boring surfaces too.
Staff Product Designer: Their work influences the craft bar across
the studio.
Their craft sets the bar in the studio.
Picks visual direction with conviction. Cuts more than they add.
Their choices become the reference for other designers
Principal Product Designer: Studio's high-water mark on craft. Craft referenced beyond the studio
Craft referenced beyond the studio; defines craft where existing visual languages don’t fit.
Sets standards that hold across project types and product categories
Visual decisions feel inevitable. Restraint and conviction in the right places.
Design Lead: Sets and holds the craft bar across projects they oversee.
Holds the craft bar on projects they oversee.
Catches drift before the client; names when work is fine but not great.
Guides designers toward a direction when there’s no clear reference to lean on.
Associate Design Director: Holds the craft bar across a portfolio of projects. Supports the studio in collectively raising its bar.
Holds the craft bar across a portfolio of projects at once.
Sets studio-wide expectations on what shipped craft should feel like.
Spots quality drift across projects on time; recovers and course corrects teams.
Design Director: Sets the studio's craft standard at the practice level.
Holds the studio’s craft standard at the practice level.
Sets what shipped Parallel craft should feel like. Their bar is the studio's bar.
Calls which work the studio takes on

Intern: Not evaluated at this level
Associate Product Designer: Forming a POV. Can say why one direction is better.
Says why one direction is better, with reasoning others can understand.
Updates the call when shown a better one.
Product Designer II: Picks well between plausible directions on clear briefs.
Holds a POV with confidence and stays open to being challenged.
Spots AI slop and pattern-matched borrowing in their own work
Calls when work is ready to ship.
Senior Product Designer: Holds a POV that travels across projects. Other designers borrow their reasoning.
Names the tradeoff the room is avoiding.
Spots AI slop and pattern-matched copying that less experienced designers miss.
Calls ship-now vs not-ready cleanly.
Staff Product Designer: Their judgment is the reference others use. SPDs take help from them for hard calls.
Names tradeoffs senior stakeholders haven’t surfaced.
Holds a strong and logical POV others can articulate even when they disagree.
Their critique on others' work changes how those designers think.
Principal Product Designer: Their POV and judgement is sought across projects.
Holds the line on judgment under client, commercial and timeline pressure.
Their tradeoff calls become studio precedent for hard decisions.
Catches systemic slop or weak thinking across the studio's work, not just one project.
Design Lead: Calibrates the designers on their team without overriding their decisions.
Strong POV that holds up across project types. Doesn't flatten other designers' POVs.
Catches systemic slop or weak thinking across the studio's work, not just one project.
Knows when to make the call themselves and when to let a designer learn by making it.
Associate Design Director: Calibrates judgment across a portfolio.
Their POV holds up across project types, client categories and team configurations.
Calls which projects need a sharper stance vs which need execution focus.
Closes systemic gaps in judgment through coaching leads & ICs
Design Director: Reshapes how the studio thinks about good work.
Their judgment is, in effect, the studio’s - shaping what it takes on and says no to.
Their feedback shapes ADDs, Leads and Principals. Calibrates studio's senior layer.
Calls strategic tradeoffs at the practice level: growth vs craft, range vs depth.

Intern: Curious about how parts fit together.
Asks how their screen connects to the rest of the product. Not just what's on it.
Reads the existing flow before designing into it. Doesn't drop work into a void.
Associate Product Designer: Sees their work as part of something larger.
Frames their work in terms of what happens before and after.
Comfortable using and extending an existing design system.
Brings up upstream and downstream impact in review, not just the screen they made.
Product Designer II: Thinks about the whole experience around the work.
Knows when the right answer isn't just an interface. (Can be process, automation, copy tweaks).
Considers second order effects of a change. Asks what shifts downstream.
Uses and contributes to shared design components that consider multiple use cases.
Senior Product Designer: Sees the whole system the work sits inside. i.e users, data, ops, business.
Ships outcomes that aren't always interfaces. Policy changes, workflow shifts, or even automations.
Identifies second, third order effects and systematic trade-offs. Evaluates the ripple effect on the system.
Designs patterns that survive future product additions without rewriting.
Staff Product Designer: Sees systems no one else in the room is
seeing yet.
Ship solutions that span product, ops, business models, comms
Pulls the room up when the screen argument isn’t the real one.
Designs for failure. Asks how the system breaks and builds the safety net in.
Principal Product Designer: Architects systems that span products, services and the studio's ways of working.
Their decisions hold up across years and product evolutions.
Designs for effects on the client’s broader business.
Finds the one change that makes the other ten unnecessary.
Design Lead: Architects systems across multiple projects they oversee.
Surfaces when the studio’s approach creates waste or contradiction.
Brings systems thinking into early client conversations. Reshapes scope.
Anticipates how decisions interact with the client's larger product, ops, business model.
Associate Design Director: Sees how projects, teams and clients interact as a system.
Designs how the studio operates. Workflow, staffing patterns, tools, rituals.
Scales quality across projects without flattening them. Each project still has its own voice.
Anticipates portfolio-level effects on capacity, talent and brand.
Design Director: Sees the studio in its market as one system. Designs how it works and evolves.
Architects the studio’s strategic direction - what it stands for, where it grows, what it stops.
Reframes the studio’s approach when the field shifts, ahead of the shift, not after.
Anticipates how today's decisions shape the studio's position in three years.

Intern: Not evaluated at this level.
Associate Product Designer: Building basic evidence literacy.
Sits in user interviews and pulls useful signal from what they hear.
Reads the analytics dashboard for their project and uses what they see in design choices.
Catches themselves when they're treating one user quote as a trend. Aware whenever biases surface, and curbs them from influencing how evidence is communicated.
Product Designer II: Brings evidence into design decisions.
Designs directly point to rationale and user insights
Calls what to test and what to ship without testing.
Reads research and analytics with a designer's eye. Connects signal to next decisions.
Distinguishes signal from noise.
Senior Product Designer: Knows what evidence the work actually needs.
Connects design choices to business levers. Activation, retention, revenue, support load.
Spots research dressing up a settled decision/opinion.
Designs the smallest test that answers the real question.
Staff Product Designer: Makes defensible calls when evidence is weak, missing or contradictory
Designs evidence approaches on novel problems when there's no standard method.
Reads what the data isn't saying as carefully as what it is.
Their evidence calls hold up under client and engineering scrutiny.
Principal Product Designer: Sets the studio’s bar for real evidence-driven design vs evidence-themed theatre.
Identifies contradictory or thin evidence, and makes defensible decisions. Other designers take it forward.
Defends the call when the client or engineering pushes back on it.
Spots when research is dressing up a decision that's already made.
Design Lead: Calls what evidence & data each project needs.
Connects evidence across projects to studio-level decisions and client business levers.
Reads signal across multiple projects and spots patterns others miss.
Associate Design Director: Sets the evidence approach for every project under them.
Keeps evidence rigour consistent across the portfolio; spots patterns to act on.
Pulls project evidence into how the studio sells; anchors how proposals get scoped.
Names what the studio runs, what it doesn’t, and what it expects clients to bring.
Design Director: Sets Parallel's stance on data and evidence driven design across the practice.
Connects evidence to how the practice positions and sells.
Reads signal across the entire portfolio and calls what it means.

Intern: Learns how to document and do a clean hand-off from peers
Hands off clean files with layers named and correct components used
Meets deadlines and delivery expectations on small scoped tasks.
Associate Product Designer: Ships scoped work well without dropping things.
Tracks the small things engineering needs. Edge cases, error states, empty states are part of the design.
Asks engineering questions early. Confirms what's buildable before designing too far in.
Engineering can build from their work without major rework.
Product Designer II: Ships their own work end-to-end. Engineering and PMs don't chase them.
Anticipates build constraints during design. Engages engineering when stuck.
Stays involved through build. Tests and catches issues before launch. Owns the work until it's live.
Senior Product Designer: Engineers & PMs treat them as thought partners.
Their early reads on feasibility reshape what gets built.
Handoff lands cleanly. Engineering can build with confidence.
Knows when to reuse an existing pattern and when a new one is worth the cost. Outcome-led, not deadline-led.
Staff Product Designer: Ships the most challenging outcomes. Engineers want them on the most challenging problems.
Engages engineering on architecture and tech tradeoffs
Engineering trusts their early reads. Their concerns reshape what gets built.
Drives outcomes where the path isn’t clear; surfaces issues early enough to change course.
Principal Product Designer: Ships work no one else in the studio could ship. Their delivery is a standard in the studio.
Designs the shipping framework for the project before code starts.
Engineering treats them as a senior collaborator. Sits in early architecture conversations and shapes the build path.
Drives shipping outcomes on work where success criteria are part of the work.
Design Lead: Ships flagship work. Owns shipping outcomes across projects they oversee.
Turns one-off shipping decisions into patterns.
Still ships flagship work themselves. Their hands-on work is where new patterns get tested.
Coaches designers through builds they haven’t faced before.
Associate Design Director: Owns shipping outcomes across the portfolio.
Holds the studio's relationship with engineering and build partners across engagements.
Calibrates how teams work with engineering. Sets expectations across projects.
Design Director: Owns shipping outcomes at the practice level.
The studio's reputation for landing work depends on them.
Reshapes how the studio works with engineering and build partners across the entire portfolio.

Intern: Curious about AI as a craft.
Comfortable using AI tools for ideation, exploration and first drafts.
Curious about how AI products work under the hood.
Associate Product Designer: AI is in their daily workflow. Spots AI slop in their own work.
Comfortable across multiple AI tools in their daily workflow.
Actively learns from their peers on various AI workflows and implements in their work.
Spots obvious AI slop in their own work before review.
Product Designer II: Designs AI-driven surfaces competently. Uses AI fluently in their own craft.
Designs AI-driven surfaces. Accounts for partial answers, latency and failure modes.
Has a working understanding of how AI products work. Context, prompts, tool use, common failure patterns.
Spots plausible-but-wrong AI output before it ships.
AI is part of how they design, prototype and iterate day to day.
Senior Product Designer: Real technical depth in both designing for AI and working AI-natively.
Real technical depth in how AI works. Context, tool use, retrieval, agents, hallucination patterns.
Strong on AI-native flow design. Inline assistance, agentic patterns, partial answers and failure modes are part of the design.
Staff Product Designer: Deepest AI fluency in the studio.
Has the deepest technical understanding of AI in the studio. Other designers learn AI from watching how they work.
Designs AI-native experiences that engineering and product borrow from.
Surfaces AI failure modes the team hasn't seen yet.
Sets the bar for what AI-fluent craft looks like inside the studio.
Principal Product Designer: Designs AI experiences that advance the field.
Designs AI-native experiences that go beyond what the studio has shipped
Defines AI quality across the studio's portfolio.
Their AI craft transfers through the team. Designers borrow their patterns on the next project.
Design Lead: Strong on both designing for AI and working AI-natively. Levels up their team.
AI is in the surfaces they ship and in how they design.
Helps PDs and SPDs on their team level up their AI fluency.
Calls AI quality across the team's work, not just their own.
Associate Design Director: Sets Parallel's AI direction across the portfolio.
Sets what AI-fluent looks like across the studio's projects.
Reads AI fluency across teams. Closes gaps through hiring, coaching and process.
Holds the bar on AI quality across projects without writing the work themselves.
Design Director: Sets Parallel's strategic direction on AI.
Reads where the field is going on AI. Positions the studio ahead of it.
Decides what kinds of AI work the studio is set up to take on and how AI fluency builds across the team.
Holds the AI craft bar at the practice level without doing the work themselves.

Intern: Shows their work clearly in internal reviews.
Walks through their thinking in internal reviews. Names what they tried and why.
Receives feedback openly and acts on it.
Associate Product Designer: Presents work confidently in internal and friendly client conversations
Receives critique without folding or getting defensive. Separates the work from themselves.
Knows when to handle a stakeholder question and when to flag to their lead.
Walks clients through their work without freezing. Answers questions in the moment.
Product Designer II: Presents work to clients and lands the recommendation.
Reads the room well. Knows when they've the client's buy-in, vs. when they quietly disengage.
Keeps the momentum going. Takes, and gives actionable feedback.
Pushes back when they should, with reasoning.
Senior Product Designer: Senior client stakeholders take their recommendations seriously
Reads multi-stakeholder client rooms. Adjusts framing for decision-makers, influencers and blockers.
Anticipates pushback. Walks in prepared. Backs up with strong logic. Offers alternatives.
Designers seek their input; their feedback lands as direction.
Staff Product Designer: Lands the toughest client conversations. Their storytelling is a reference.
Walks in and closes the conversations the project has been avoiding. Re-scoping, pushing back, sequencing tradeoffs no one wants to call.
Trusted by senior client stakeholders as a thinking partner, not just a vendor.
Their judgment transfers through the team without them being a manager.
Principal Product Designer: Lands the hardest studio-wide conversations, including pushing back on attractive-but-weak work.
They get pulled into strategy calls beyond the design work.
Sought across projects for sharpening. Their critique and POV is the one designers actively want.
Raises the studio's bar of design conversation. Decisions get sharper around them.
Design Lead: Carries senior client relationships across multiple projects.
Lands difficult client conversations on behalf of the team. Re-scoping, holding the line.
Sets how the team presents and pushes back.
Runs critiques that produce sharper work, not louder rooms. Their feedback is sought.
Associate Design Director: Trusted as the studio's voice in CXO-level rooms.
Carries senior client relationships across multiple engagements. Trusted as the studio's voice in CXO-level rooms.
Lands the most difficult client conversations on behalf of teams. Holding the line, walking away when needed.
Calibrates how the studio gives and receives feedback. Sets the bar on critique culture.
Design Director: Carries the studio's most senior client relationships. Their POV is sought at CXO level on strategy.
Lands the practice's hardest commercial conversations. Pricing, positioning, choosing what to bet on.
Coaches ADDs and senior ICs. Their feedback shapes the studio's senior layer over years.
Their voice carries inside the studio. People align around the sharpness of the reasoning.

Intern: Curious about who the work is for and why.
Asks who the work is for and what they're trying to do.
Associate Product Designer: Thinks about who uses the product and why.
Reads the client's product to understand what it does and who uses it, before designing into it.
Asks why a feature matters before how it should look.
Makes an active effort to start building product sense beyond design skills
Product Designer II: Connects design choices to user outcomes.
Thinks about the client’s product and where their own work fits into it.
Asks why before what.
Connects design choices to user outcomes (activation, task completion, retention)
Questions how a decision affects business outcomes.
Senior Product Designer: Thinks like a product owner.
Fluent in the client's business model, user economics and product strategy.
Connects design to conversion, retention, ARR; knows which metrics they’ll move.
Hears what the client cares about underneath what they ask for.
Recognises 'competitor-copying' asks, and handles well.
Staff Product Designer: Operates with the client's strategy in their head.
Spots strategic gaps the client hasn't yet articulated
Their work moves revenue, retention and market position.
Their POV on the client's product gets taken seriously inside the client's own org.
Principal Product Designer: Thinks like a senior client leader. Their POV is taken seriously beyond design.
Thinks with revenue, moat and market position directly, without translating from design
Surfaces strategic risks and opportunities the client team hasn’t named.
Recognises patterns across the studio’s client portfolio.
Design Lead: Operates with multiple clients' businesses in their head.
Fluent in the business of every client they oversee, switching context smoothly.
Reframes a project when it's solving the wrong problem. Catches it before it costs the team weeks.
Their work moves client outcomes at flagship-project scale.
Associate Design Director: Thinks across multiple client businesses at strategic depth.
Their POV shapes the studio’s commercial direction. Calls which clients to invest in, which to let go
Their work at portfolio scale shapes studio outcomes - revenue, reputation, positioning.
Design Director: Thinks with the field. Calls the studio's strategic position.
Operates at the level of the field. Where design is going, where the market is going, where the studio fits.
Calls the studio's strategic position. What Parallel stands for, who it serves, what it says no to.
Connects work, people, brand and commercial into a coherent direction. Their POV is the studio's POV.

Design Lead: Coaches designers in the studio. Curates a custom growth trajectory for coachees under them
Associate Design Director: Develops the studio's senior layer. Raises the bar with every hire.
Design Director: Shapes how Parallel identifies and grows exceptional talent.

Design Lead: Designs each direct report's growth deliberately. Knows where they're reaching, where they're stuck, what stretches them next.
On projects, lifts the PDs and SPDs they work with. Coaches in the moment, in critique, in the work.
Runs 1:1s that move work and growth, and not just roles.
Supports collective growth and upskilling in the studio. Keeps the floor curious, and continuously learning to raise the overall craft bar.
Hires for their team. Interviews PDs and SPDs, calibrates them against the studio's bar.
Associate Design Director: Manages DLs and senior ICs. Calibrates them on craft, judgment and leadership.
Hires for the senior layer. Each hire raises the bar, not just fills a gap.
Coaches in a way that moves work and growth, and not just roles.

Names where the studio needs to grow as a whole. Craft, judgment, AI fluency, wherever the gap is. Guides DLs and senior ICs in running the learning and conversations that recalibrate the mid and junior layers.
Design Director: Designs the studio's growth philosophy. What we promote for, what we hire for, what we coach.
The studio's reputation for developing designers rests on them. Designers want to come here because of how people grow here.

influence
ic.note: Legs for IC: UX Design & Ideation, Visual Craft, Product Sense (Taste & Judgement moved to the head 2026-07-06 per Shreya). Applied to Intern and Associate PD as well.
